coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of novel ternary g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts and their visible-light-induced activities in removal of various contaminants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Shahedi Asl, Mehdi
Ahmadi, Zohre
Ghosh, Srabanti
coredata.dc:description:
Different visible-light-driven nanocomposites were fabricated via combination of g-C3N4 (CN) with SiC and carbon dots (C-Dots) in the current research work. The features of photocatalysts were investigated by various analyses. The photocatalytic abilities were investigated by removal four aquatic pollutants. The outcomes demonstrated that the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) system has excellent photoability in the elimination of MB, fuchsine, RhB, and CR, which was about 26.9, 20.9, 24.4, and 15.2-folds premier than the single CN, respectively. The boosted photodegradation performance was related to the efficient separation of charges and high visible-light absorption, because of coupling CN with SiC and C-Dots. It was confirmed that the h+ and
O2 − plays essential roles in photocatalytic abilities. In our belief, this research work represents a novel method for the production of ternary metal-free nanocomposites based on CN, which could efficiently solve environmental crises.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots
Visible-light-induced photocatalyst
Electron–hole separation
Photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge University of Mohaghegh Ardabili for providing the research facility and funding to carry out this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The image for synthesis of the CN/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts.
The XRD patterns of CN, SiC, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) systems.
(a) EDS spectra of the CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples.
(a) TEM, and (b) HRTEM related to the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and XPS spectra of: (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Si 2p, and (e) O 1s in the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) systems. (b) UV-vis DR spectra of the CN, CN/SiC, and CN/SiC/C-Dots samples.
TGA diagrams of the CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposites with various C-Dots volumes.
(a, b) RhB degradation and (c) the degradation rate constants on the as-fabricated materials.
UV–vis spectra for RhB elimination under visible-light illumination on the (a) CN, (b) CN/SiC (40 %), and (c) CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples.
(a) PL spectra, (b) EIS results, and (c) Photocurrent spectra for the as-fabricated systems.
BET analyses of the CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) samples.
(a) Mott–Schottky curve for SiC. (b) Scheme for photocatalytic reaction over the CN/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts.
The RhB elimination rate constants on the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) system in attendance of several trapping agents.
(a) Durability of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst. (b) XRD patterns and (c) FT-IR spectra of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite before and after photocatalysis.
The elimination rate constants of (a) RhB, (b) fuchsine, (c) MB, and (d) CR on the as-prepared photocatalysts.
The textural features of CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of novel ternary g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts and their visible-light-induced activities in removal of various contaminants

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Department of Organic and Inorganic Chemistry, Universidad De Alcala, Madrid, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Different visible-light-driven nanocomposites were fabricated via combination of g-C3N4 (CN) with SiC and carbon dots (C-Dots) in the current research work. The features of photocatalysts were investigated by various analyses. The photocatalytic abilities were investigated by removal four aquatic pollutants. The outcomes demonstrated that the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) system has excellent photoability in the elimination of MB, fuchsine, RhB, and CR, which was about 26.9, 20.9, 24.4, and 15.2-folds premier than the single CN, respectively. The boosted photodegradation performance was related to the efficient separation of charges and high visible-light absorption, because of coupling CN with SiC and C-Dots. It was confirmed that the h+ and
O2 − plays essential roles in photocatalytic abilities. In our belief, this research work represents a novel method for the production of ternary metal-free nanocomposites based on CN, which could efficiently solve environmental crises.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic dyes are considerably utilized in printing and dyeing industries, like paper, fabric, cosmetics, and leather, due to the global demand. These dyes are generally applied as colorants with high molecular weight and complex structures, which are degradation-resistant, potential carcinogens, and soluble in water [1–3]. As a result, dye-containing effluents threaten human’s health and straightly destroy the ecological balance of the water body. These days, different researches have been focused on sewage treatment, as a hot spot of society, and the degradation of contaminants in water bodies by environmentally-friendly novel methods [4,5]. For the survival of all living organisms and human course, it is necessary to remove the dyes in a high-efficient and eco-friendly path. Lately, photocatalytic processes based on semiconductors introduced an attractive and environmentally safe procedure for water treatment and environmental remediation. Furthermore, the challenge in the field of photocatalysis is designing stable and effective visible-light-driven photocatalysts [6–12].

g-C3N4 (graphitic carbon nitride; abbreviated as CN), has been applied as a semiconductor for contaminant removals owing to its chemical stability against organic solvents, basic, and acids, high thermal durability, and having small band gap [13–15]. Nevertheless, its usage as photocatalyst is restricted by small specific surface area, low quantum efficiency, quick recombination of photogenerated carriers, and the relatively medium region of photoresponse [16,17]. So, owing to the mentioned drawbacks, the photoactivity of single CN is poor. Abundant suggestions have been made to ameliorate the photoactivity of pristine CN such as anchoring other semiconductors, morphology controlling, heteroatoms doping, and integration with carbon nanomaterials [18–33].

Pursuant to wide investigations, the separation efficiency of the charges and the solar energy harvesting ability can be remarkably increased via heterojunction formation among CN and the semiconductors with suitable band gaps [34,35]. To obtain the mentioned items, CN/SiC and CN/C-Dots heterostructures were fabricated, which exhibited increased ability in the photocatalytic applications [36–42]. As promising semiconductor, SiC is a common nonmetallic photocatalyst with a suitable energy gap (2.48 eV), suitable energy levels, and adequate chemical and thermal durability, which enables to be a fascinating visible-light-driven photocatalyst. Furthermore, its supreme e−/h+ pair mobility can shuttle the produced charges rapidly before recombination. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) potentials of SiC make it appropriate to combine with CN to fabricate effective photocatalysts to be activated under visible light [38,43]. Also, due to the up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) property of carbon dots (C-Dots), which was completely proved [44], they convert the larger wavelengths to the smaller wavelengths of the utilized light via consecutive absorbing photon with low energy and emitting high energy photons [44–48]. About UCPL feature of C-Dots, it should be explained that under the second-order diffraction light of wavelength λ/2 coexisting in the excitation light of wavelength λ is responsible for the seen UCPL in C-Dots [49]. Besides, C-Dots acts as an electron acceptor and donor and leading to enhanced movement of electrons between components of multicomponent photocatalysts. So, it can be derived that SiC and C-Dots nanoparticles are proper for coupling with CN to fabricate impressive photocatalysts under visible-light illumination. It can be concluded that it is favorable to improve a multi heterojunction nanocomposite with CN, SiC, and C-Dots to achieve greatly boosted photocatalyst under visible light.

In this study, the ternary metal-free CN/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposites were constructed by an easy procedure. These composites demonstrated superior abilities in the removal of fuchsine, RhB, MB, and CR from water. Relevant photocatalysts were characterized, and a probable photocatalytic mechanism for transfer of charge carrier was presented.

The phase characterization was studied by a XRD (Philips Xpert). Microstructural and chemical investigations were done using a FESEM and EDX systems (LEO 1430VP operating at 15 kV). The TEM and HRTEM images were used for further microstructural studies by Tecnai G2 30ST (FEI). A UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to provide the related spectra (Cecile 9000). The FT-IR and PL spectra were done on PerkinElmer Spectrum RX I and fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. N2 adsorption–desorption was done on a Belsorp Mini II system. The DRS was plotted by a Scinco 4100 spectrophotometer. The TGA were done via Linseis STA PT 1000 system. The photoelectrochemical characterizations were realized by a standard three-electrode. More information has been represented in our previous research papers [30].
The CN was constructed by a formerly presented manner [33]. Briefly, melamine powder was heated at 500 °C for 120 min then calcined to 520 °C for 120 min with a ramp rate of 10 °C min−1.

For fabrication of the binary CN/SiC (40 %) photocatalyst, in which 40 % is the weight percentage of SiC, 0.2 g of as-received SiC ultrafine particles (Xuzhou Hongwu Co., China) was added to 0.75 g of melamine and powdered with mortar for 30 min. After that, the resulting powder mixture was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and dried in air oven at 60 °C. Finally, to obtain the binary CN/SiC photocatalyst, the resulting powder annealed in air ambient at 500 °C for 120 min and then annealed to 520 °C for 120 min.

Usually, C-Dots can be fabricated through various procedures like hydrothermal, laser ablation, ultrasonic, and microwave procedures [50–52]. In this work, among the synthesis methods, the C-Dots were synthesized via microwave method [26]. For construction of C-Dots with concentration of 5 mg/mL, 3 g urea and 3 g citric acid were introduced to 10 mL water and then was illuminated for 5 min in a domestic microwave oven (750 W). During this reaction, the color of solution is changed from a colorless liquid to a dark-brown solid. The obtained solid was put at 60 °C for 60 min in an oven to eliminate the volatile molecules. Thereupon, the obtained powder of C-Dots was dissolved in 180 mL of water. Afterwards, the agglomerated particles of the C-Dots solution was eliminate through centrifuging for 10 min (7500 rmin−1). The obtained brown aqueous solution remained stable for a long time [53].

Finally, for the construction of the ternary CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite, where 0.25 mL is amount of the applied C-Dots, exactly, 0.4 g of the CN/SiC (40 %) and 0.25 mL of C-Dots were ultrasound in 100 mL water for 5 min. Finally, the obtained suspension dried at 80 °C for one day (Scheme 1 ).

The photoactivities were measured at 25 °C under visible-light equipped by a 50 W LED lamp for eliminations of MB, RhB, CR, and fuchsine by recording the characteristic absorption peaks at 664, 553, 496, and 540 nm, respectively. The other conditions were entirely described elsewhere [26].

The XRD analysis was utilized to study the crystal structure and phase purity of the nanocomposites (Fig. 1 ). The single CN exhibits two clear peaks at 13.1° and 27.7° (JCPDS 87-1526). The characteristic peak located at 13.1° was related to (100), as heptazine units, and the one locating at 27.7° could be assigned to (002) plane, as the interlayer stacking of aromatic segments [26,33]. The main XRD peaks of the SiC powder match with α-SiC and 6H-SiC structures according to JCPDS 01-072-0018 and 01-070-2550, respectively [39,54]. Also, in the pattern of binary CN/SiC (40 %) photocatalyst, in addition the CN characteristic peaks, the peaks located at 71.9°, 60.1°, 41.4°, 35.7° assigned to the (311), (220), (200), (111) of hexagonal SiC crystalline planes, as main phase of α-SiC (JCPDS 01-072-0018) [39]. A weak peak marked with the red circle is ascribed to the stacking faults, which can be seen at 33.7° [38,39]. Also the peak located in 2θ = 37.1° related to phase minor of 6H-SiC (JCPDS 01-070-2550) [54]. After coupling with 0.25 mL C-Dots on the CN/SiC (40 %) nanocomposite, the XRD pattern of CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst exhibits no trace of C-Dots nanoparticles, which is related to the low volume of C-Dots adhered on the photocatalyst [55].

The EDS was used to specify the presence of elements in the as-fabricated CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) and they are demonstrated in Fig. 2 . The EDS of CN depicted the presence of N and C elements. The EDS of CN/SiC (40 %) photocatalyst shows the existence of C, N, and Si without any impurities. As envisaged, we obviously saw the peaks for C, N, Si, and O elements in the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) system. The O element, observed in the EDS, is due to the existence of several functional groups like CO, OH, and OCO in C-Dots [29–32]. Hence, the outcomes determined that C-Dots have adhered successfully on the surface of the CN/SiC (40 %) photocatalyst.

The microstructures and morphology of as-fabricated CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite were recognized via TEM and HRTEM, and the outcomes are depicted in Fig. 3 (a, b). Fig. 3a displays the irregular nanoplate morphology for as-resultant CN. It is observable that some dark particles located on the CN sheets, which are attributed to SiC and C-Dots components. Finally, the HRTEM image (Fig. 3b) displays three various lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.325 [56], 0.250 [38], and 0.320 nm [33], corresponding to the (002) plane of CN, the (111) plane of SiC, and the (002) plane of C-Dots, respectively. Thus, the different lattice fringes and the structure of the composite proved successful preparation of the ternary nanocomposite.

XPS spectra were recorded to examine the chemical surface states of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite. As observed in the full survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a, the main component elements for the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst are C, N, O, and Si, which consistent with their chemical compositions. The carbon element was attributed to the CN and C-Dots components. According to Fig. 4b, the peak of C 1s located at 284.6 eV is related to bonded carbon at sp2 CC, ascribed to C element in the CN plane [55,57]. Also, the peak observed at 285.6 eV is attributed to COH group, which may be due the remarkable interaction among CN and C-Dots [55]. Also the C peak observed at 288.5 eV could be ascribed to the sp2 carbon presented in the NCN from CN [55, 57]. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 4c shows two peaks at 398.2 and 400.4 eV, which related to NCN and N(C)3, respectively, belonging to CN [58]. For Si 2p spectrum in Fig. 4d, the peak at 100.5 eV is attributed to the SiC bond [59]. At last, the spectrum of O 1s displays three peaks at 531.8, 533.02, and 534.1 eV, which are related to the chemical environments of COH/COC, CO, HO groups, respectively (Fig. 4c] [60]. Hence, the XPS analysis, in accordance with the EDX, HRTEM, and XRD analyses, confirms adhering of SiC, and C-Dots on the surface of CN.

Fig. 5 a shows the FT-IR spectra of CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts. For the CN powder, the wide absorption peak at 2900-3600 cm−1 area is related to the vibrational stretching modes of OH and NH group, respectively, which are related to surface adsorbed water molecules and uncondensed amino groups. The peaks in the region of 1200−1600 cm−1 are ascribed to the CN heterocycles stretching modes, while the peak at 809 cm−1 is the breathing mode of heptazine [61]. For the CN/SiC (40 %) nanocomposite, a sharp band at 858 cm−1 can be shown, which ascribed to the Si-C stretching vibration mode [39]. For the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) system, the peaks at the 1650 cm−1 and 1350−1460 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of CH2 and carbonyl groups, respectively, which were ascribed to the C-Dots [62–64].

The absorption area of light is very necessary for the photocatalytic performance in visible light. So, the UV–vis DRS were obtained and exhibited in Fig. 5b. As observed, the absorption edge of the single CN is positioned at 480 nm, related to the energy gap of CN [61]. For the CN/SiC nanocomposites, by increasing SiC amount, the absorptions in visible region are significantly improved in comparison with the single CN [39]. Finally, as can be seen, after deposition of C-Dots on the CN/SiC (40 %) nanocomposite, the absorbance was enhanced in visible region, owing to enhanced harvesting ability of C-Dots.

To study actual values of CN and thermal stability of the fabricated nanocomposites, TGA was used. As can be illustrated in Fig. 5, the nanocomposites do not have remarkable weight loss up to 400 °C. The small weight losses are assigned to evaporation of water molecules kept from purification step. Also, it can be seen from Fig. 6 that the burning of CN happens in the temperature region of 490−680 °C, which is in agreement with the literatures [33,61]. Whereas, the thermo stability of the photocatalysts diminished after integration with SiC and C-Dots, when compared with the single CN. In the nanocomposites, the adhered SiC and C-Dots particles on the CN could act as active places in order to adsorb the surrounding O2, leading to quickly oxidation of CN part of the samples at lower temperatures than the single CN [65,66]. Based on this analysis, when the temperature was higher than ∼600 °C, no obvious mass loss occurs at TG curves. Moreover, the weight percentage of CN in the as-fabricated photocatalysts was determined from the weight loss after complete combustion of photocatalysts and they displayed in Fig. 6.

The degradation behavior of various as-fabricated nanocomposites was investigated by RhB, as a usual dye contaminant, on visible light (Fig. 7 a, b). At first, the removal reaction was accomplished in the absence of nanocomposite to discover the role of photolysis. The outcome illuminated that RhB self-degradation is negligible and it is comparatively stable. As observed, in existence of the single CN, only 33 % of RhB was removed after 90 min. The removal rate of RhB strongly enhanced when the binary CN/SiC and ternary CN/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts were applied. The data revealed that compared to other ternary photocatalysts, the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst displays the best ability. It is well known that degradation of most contaminants fits to first-order kinetic model. So, the values of apparent rate constants were computed and are displayed in Fig. 7c. The rate constant is found to be 316 × 10−4 min-1 over the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst. It is quite proof that the rate constant over the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) sample is almost 24.4 and 2.2-folds higher than the bare CN and CN/SiC nanocomposite with optimum value of SiC, which is 40 %.

The UV–vis spectral variations of RhB solution in the presence of various nanocomposites were exhibited in Fig. 8(a–c). The intensity of RhB solution’s absorbance gradually decreased at 553 nm with illumination time. In existence of the CN and CN/SiC (40 %) nanocomposite, the photocatalytic efficiency was about 33 and 52 % after 90 min irradiation, respectively, which confirms partial photoability of this binary nanocomposite. Whereas, absorption spectrum was almost decreased in the existence of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite within the same time, demonstrating that the photoactivity of the as-fabricated ternary sample is much greater than those of the binary and single samples.

Forasmuch PL emission mostly derives from the recombination of e−/h+ pairs, so PL spectroscopy is a simple technique to check the separation efficiency of these charges in a photocatalyst. Fig. 9 a exhibits the PL spectra of CN, SiC, C-Dots, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts. The single CN demonstrates a strong PL emission peak located at 530 nm. It can be found that the PL spectrum of CN shows intensive emission band centered at 530 nm. This sharp peak is related to the band-band PL phenomenon with the energy of light almost equal to the energy gap of CN. In real, the band-band PL signal is ascribed to excitonic PL, which outcomes from the n→π* electronic transition involving lone pairs of N atoms presented in CN [67–69]. As a result, it was deduced that owing to strong interactions among CN, SiC, and C-Dots, the PL intensity of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) sample was weaken, denoting more impressive prevention of these charges from recombination.

The semicircle radius on the Nyquist plot reflects the charge transfer, carrier recombination, and conductivity during the photocatalytic process. So, EIS measurements in Fig. 9b were conducted to study the separation efficiency of the charges. The arc diameter of the CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts demonstrates a declining trend. Among samples, the arc diameter of CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst is the smallest, which corresponds to the best conductivity for the charges. These factors are leading to an impressive separation of charge carriers during the photocatalytic process, which improves the photoability. The mentioned outcomes are well agreement with the photocatalytic performances. Finally, to present major proof to support the impressive separation of the charges in these systems, the transient photocurrent responses were determined. Fig. 9c displays stable and reproducible photocurrent responses to on-off cycles in the photocatalysts. Especially, the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst demonstrated the highest photocurrent response under visible light in comparison with those of CN and CN/SiC (40 %) photocatalysts. This increased photocurrent response can be related to the presence of a strong interface among CN, SiC, and C-Dots components in the ternary photocatalyst, where charge carriers could be impressively separated. The vast transfer efficiency of charge carriers leads to the dramatically enhanced photoability of CN/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts.

To investigation the porous structure of the photocatalysts, the textural analysis was provided and exhibited in Fig. 10 . It is clear that the sorption isotherms of CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) powders display the main characteristics of mesoporous structure by type-IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops. The textural features of the mentioned samples were presented in Table 1 . As we observe, the BET surface area (SBET) for the CN, CN/SiC (40 %), and CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalysts are 14.6, 18.1, and 24.4 m2 g−1, respectively. Compared with that of the CN, the SBET amounts of binary and ternary photocatalysts have negligibility increased. The slightly increased SBET is able to display more active sites to dyes molecules, which leads to increase photocatalytic ability. Therefore, it can be derived that one of the major factors affecting the photocatalytic ability is the improved surface area. Finally, as proved by the PL, EIS, photocurrent density, UV–vis, and BET outcomes, the effective suppression of e−/h+ pairs from the recombination, efficient transfer of the charges, increased absorption of visible light, and the slightly increased SBET played significant duty in increasing the photocatalytic ability.

Mott-Schottky measurement is generally utilized to find the flat band potentials and type of semiconductors. It is observed that the slope of the Mott-Schottky plot for SiC is positive, therefore the as-fabricated SiC is an n-type semiconductor [70]. From Fig. 11 a, the EF of SiC is specified to be −0.37 eV vs. Ag/AgCl, which is converted to −0.17 eV vs. NHE [71,72]. Commonly, the CB level of n-type materials is located 0.1 V less than the flat band [73]. Henceforward, the ECB of the SiC was obtained −0.27 eV. In addition, the EVB of SiC is positioned at +2.21 eV, according to Eg = 2.48 eV and experimental formula of EVB = Eg + ECB. Whereas, the flat band potential of n-type CN has been presented in the previous research work [30]. Herein, a mechanism for the improved photoactivity in the CN/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts was proposed pursuant to the above-presented outcomes. We proposed a type-II heterojunction mechanism for the degradation process on the CN/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposite. As demonstrated in Fig. 11b, the boosted photoability of the nanocomposite in visible area is assigned to two parameters. The first parameter related to CN and SiC, which are semiconductors with narrow band gaps, permitting to trap more visible-light photons to produce more e−/h+ pairs. The second parameter is related to features of C-Dots, which can absorb long wavelengths to produce short wavelengths. Pursuant to this explanation, after excitation of CN and SiC semiconductors by up-converted photons with C-Dots, the electrons in the CB of CN are moved to the CB of SiC via C-Dots as mediators, while h+ is transformed from the VB of SiC to CN. Meantime, C-Dots act as electron source to trap electrons from CN. Thus, an impressive e−/h+ pair separation happens over the prepared CN/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts, as proved by PL spectra and photocurrent density. As shown in Fig. 11b, the electrons on CN, SiC and C-Dots react with O2 to generate
O2 −, as major reactive species, because the CB levels of these components are more negative than that of O2/
O2 − (−0.33 eV/NHE) [17]. Meanwhile, h+ gathered at the VB of CN are insufficient to react with OH− or H2O species to generate
OH, since the VB level of CN (1.57 eV) is less positive than E° (H2O/
OH = +2.72 eV) and E°(OH−/O
H = +2.69 eV) [17,74]. So, the presented holes in the VB of CN could directly degrade dye pollutants.

To determine the major active species in degradation reaction, various scavengers were applied. As depicted in Fig. 12 , in the existence of AO, BQ, and 2-PrOH, the degradation rate constants were diminished from 316 × 10−4 min-1 to 32.4 × 10−4 min-1, 10.1 × 10−4 min-1, and 206 × 10−4 min-1, respectively. Hence, owing to the considerable effect of AO and BQ on the photoability of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst, it can be deduced that in the elimination reaction of RhB,
O2- and h+ were main reactive species involved compared with
OH.

In addition to excellent degradation performance, a photocatalyst is suggested to be durable under the reaction conditions. So to further recognize its superiority, we also evaluated the cycling performance of CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite, as optimum photocatalyst, for the elimination of RhB. After each cycle, the used CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst was rinsed with ethyl alcohol and water, and then dried in oven for reuse. As depicted in Fig. 13 , the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst exhibits superior stability with little loss in the degradation efficiency during four consecutive cycles. Furthermore, to confirm the durability, the XRD patterns of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst before and after 4th recovery were examined (Fig. 13b). It can be obviously seen that the structure and phase of the photocatalyst is stable, which expresses that the nanocomposite has an excellent durability during the photocatalytic process. Besides, the functional groups of the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst before and after photocatalysis were evaluated via FT-IR analysis and the outcomes are demonstrated in Fig. 13c, which prove the structural durability during the removal process. Therefore, we believe that it can be selected as stable photocatalyst for various applications such as hydrogen production, environmental contaminant removal, and CO2 reduction.

To study the capability of the ternary CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) photocatalyst for elimination various contaminants, the degradations of fuchsine, MB, and CR were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 14(a–d). As seen, the kinetic constants over the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) nanocomposite for eliminations of various dyes are premier than the CN and CN/SiC (40 %) photocatalysts, which is about 24.4, 20.9, 26.9, and 15.2 folds as high as the CN, and 2.2, 2.2, 3.6, and 2.1-folds premier than the CN/SiC (40 %) nanocomposite for removal of RhB, fuchsine, MB, and CR, respectively. Thus, the CN/SiC/C-Dots (0.25 mL) sample is a favorable selection for removing various industrial pollutants.

Briefly, ternary CN/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposites were constructed by an easy way and were adopted to characterize the photocatalysts by various techniques. The constructed CN/SiC/C-Dots photocatalysts illustrated much enhanced photoactivity in removal of three cationic (RhB, MB, fuchsine) and one anionic (CR) pollutants compared to the bulk CN and binary CN/SiC (40 %) nanocomposite upon visible light. The improved absorption ability of visible light and impressive separation of the charges due to coupling CN with SiC and C-Dots benefited to obtain superior photoability. Accordingly, it was proved that the CN/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposite is a type of impressive visible-light-induced sample for removal of several pollutants in our environment.

Recently, photocatalytic processes based on semiconductors introduced an attractive and environmentally safe procedure for water treatment and environmental remediation. G-C3N4 (graphitic carbon nitride) has been applied as a semiconductor for contaminant removals owing to many appealing features. Pursuant to wide investigations, the separation efficiency of the charges and the solar energy harvesting ability can be remarkably increased via heterojunction formation among g-C3N4 and the semiconductors with suitable band gaps. As promising semiconductor, SiC is a common nonmetallic photocatalyst with a suitable energy gap (2.48 eV), suitable energy levels, and adequate chemical and thermal durability, which enables to be a fascinating visible-light-driven photocatalyst. The valence band and conduction band potentials of SiC make it appropriate to combine with g-C3N4 to fabricate effective photocatalysts to be activated under visible light. Also, due to the up-conversion photoluminescence property of C-Dots, they convert the larger wavelengths to the smaller wavelengths of the utilized light via consecutive absorbing photon with low energy and emitting high energy photons Besides, C-Dots acts as an electron acceptor and donor and leading to enhanced movement of electrons between components of multicomponent photocatalysts. So, it can be derived that SiC and C-Dots nanoparticles are proper for coupling with g-C3N4 to fabricate impressive photocatalysts under visible-light illumination. It can be concluded that it is favorable to improve a multi heterojunction nanocomposite with g-C3N4, SiC, and C-Dots to achieve greatly boosted photocatalyst under visible light. In this study, the ternary metal-free g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposites were constructed by an easy procedure. These composites demonstrated superior abilities in the removal of fuchsine, RhB, MB, and CR from water. Relevant photocatalysts were characterized, and a probable photocatalytic mechanism for transfer of charge carrier was presented.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge University of Mohaghegh Ardabili for providing the research facility and funding to carry out this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112431.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
